title: 28 Meskerem — St. Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob & The Assembly
narrative:
title": "28 Meskerem — St. Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob & The Assembly

narrative": "Meskerem

The Holy Patriarchs Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob

St. Chariton the Confessor, Abbot

St. Isaac of Harpe, Desert Father

The twenty-eighth day of Meskerem brings the Holy Patriarchs — Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — into the new year's assembly, and their arrival in the first month of the new cycle has a quality distinct from their appearance on the twenty-eighth of Nehase. In Nehase they came at the year's ending, as part of the great recapitulation. Here in Meskerem they come at the beginning, and they come as the founders of everything that follows: Abraham who went out in faith, Isaac who lay down on the mountain and rose again, Jacob who wrestled with the Angel of the Lord and received both a wound and a name. The new year begins with these three because the faith that the year will test is the faith that they established — a faith that moves into the unknown, that offers what is most precious, that does not let go of the divine presence until it has received the blessing.

St. Chariton the Confessor was a third-century pilgrim from Iconium who was captured by bandits on his way to Jerusalem, miraculously delivered when the bandit chief was killed by a snake (and the remaining bandits, terrified, fled), and who used the bandit cave as the nucleus for a monastic community — the first of three lavras he would found in the Judean desert. His story is one of the tradition's most beautiful examples of providential conversion: the planned pilgrimage becomes an unplanned captivity becomes an unplanned liberation becomes an unintended monastery. God's purposes for Chariton bore no resemblance to Chariton's purposes for himself, and yet God's purposes were better. On the twenty-eighth day of the new year, Chariton stands as the counsel against overly determined plans: hold the year's intentions loosely, because the cave in which you are detained may turn out to be the monastery in which you are formed.

St. Isaac of Harpe, the desert father whose name connects him to the great Isaac-tradition of the Syrian and Egyptian monasticism, brings the contemplative note into the twenty-eighth day's assembly alongside the Patriarchs and the reluctant-monk Chariton. Isaac of Harpe is associated with the tradition of the harp as an instrument of prayer — the Psalter sung in the cell, the sacred songs that David composed and the monks inherited, the music that is not entertainment but intercession. On the twenty-eighth day of Meskerem, in a month that has been filled with the extraordinary music of Maskal and Enkutatash, Isaac of Harpe reminds the community that the music of the great feasts descends from the music of the cell — that the liturgical splendor of the public celebration is sustained by the unceasing private prayer of those who sing in the hidden place.

Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Chariton, and Isaac of Harpe together compose the twenty-eighth day's meditation on the faith that builds across generations and circumstances. The Patriarchs built it across centuries; Chariton built it across the unexpected conversion of a robbers' den; Isaac of Harpe built it note by note in the solitude of the cell. These are the three modes of the same faith: the dynastic, the providential, and the contemplative. The new year's community receives them as its teachers — the fathers who model the faith that makes new years worth beginning, the monk who trusts God's detours, and the singer who sustains the whole enterprise in the silence between the feasts.
